news_summary: Today’s space news highlights groundbreaking discoveries about the universe’s origins, the potential role of meteors in sparking life on Earth, exciting lunar exploration plans, and new insights into the interactions between dying stars and giant planets.
news_summary_fr: L'actualité spatiale d'aujourd'hui met en avant des découvertes révolutionnaires sur les origines de l'univers, le rôle potentiel des météores dans l'apparition de la vie sur Terre, des projets passionnants d'exploration lunaire, ainsi que de nouvelles perspectives sur les interactions entre les étoiles mourantes et les planètes géantes.
news_summary_es: Las noticias espaciales de hoy destacan descubrimientos revolucionarios sobre los orígenes del universo, el posible papel de los meteoritos en el origen de la vida en la Tierra, emocionantes planes de exploración lunar y nuevos conocimientos sobre las interacciones entre las estrellas moribundas y los planetas gigantes.

articles.title: Students found a star from the dawn of the universe drifting into the Milky Way
articles.summary: A group of undergraduate students stumbled into a cosmic time capsule—one of the oldest stars ever discovered—while combing through massive astronomy datasets. What began as a class project quickly turned into a breakthrough when they spotted an extraordinarily “pristine” star made almost entirely of hydrogen and helium, hinting it formed near the dawn of the universe.
articles.content:
A group of undergraduate students at the University of Chicago has identified one of the oldest known stars in the universe using data from the Sloan Digital Sky Survey (SDSS). The star did not originate in our galaxy. Instead, it formed in a nearby companion galaxy and later moved into the Milky Way.

The discovery was made by ten students enrolled in the university's "Field Course in Astrophysics," taught by Professor Alex Ji, deputy Project Scientist for SDSS-V, along with graduate teaching assistants Hillary Andales and Pierre Thibodeaux.

How Big Data Led to a Major Discovery

SDSS is a global collaboration involving more than 75 scientific institutions and has been operating for 25 years. Its mission is to make large astronomical datasets publicly available so researchers and students alike can explore them. In its current phase, the project uses robotic instruments to collect spectra from millions of objects across the sky, helping scientists study how stars, black holes, and galaxies evolve over time.

In Ji's class, students worked directly with SDSS data. Over several weeks, they examined thousands of stars from the latest survey results, searching for unusual candidates. From this effort, they selected 77 stars for closer study during a planned observing trip.

A Spring Break Trip That Changed Everything

The group traveled to Carnegie Science's Las Campanas Observatory in Chile during Spring Break, where they used the Magellan Inamori Kyocera Echelle (MIKE) instrument on the Magellan telescopes. Their first observing session took place on March 21st, 2025. The second star they examined that night, labeled SDSSJ0715-7334, quickly stood out.

"We found it the first night, and it completely changed our plans for the course," Ji said.

Originally, the plan was to observe each target for about 10 minutes. After realizing how unusual this star was, the students spent three hours studying it the following night.

"I was looking at that camera the whole night to make sure it was working," said Natalie Orrantia, one of the students involved in the discovery.

An "Ancient Immigrant" Star From Another Galaxy

The star turned out to be extraordinarily pure, consisting almost entirely of hydrogen and helium. This chemical makeup indicates it formed very early in cosmic history, making it one of the oldest stars ever observed.

Further analysis revealed that the star was not born in the Milky Way. Instead, it formed in the Large Magellanic Cloud, the Milky Way's largest companion galaxy, and later migrated into our galaxy billions of years ago. Because of its origin and age, Ji described it as an "ancient immigrant."

"This ancient immigrant gives us an unprecedented look at conditions in the early universe," said Ji. "Big data projects like SDSS make it possible for students to get directly involved in these important discoveries."

Record-Low Metallicity Reveals Early Universe Origins

Astronomers use the term "metals" to describe elements heavier than hydrogen and helium. A star's "metallicity" refers to how much of these heavier elements it contains. SDSSJ0715-7334 has just 0.005 percent of the metals found in the Sun, making it the most metal-poor star ever observed, more than twice as metal-poor as the previous record holder.

"We analyzed the star for a large swath of elements, and the abundances are quite low for all of them," said Ha Do, another student on the team.

Low metallicity is a key indicator of age. Elements heavier than hydrogen and helium are created in supernova explosions. A star with very few of these elements must have formed before most supernovae occurred, meaning it likely belongs to the earliest generations of stars in the universe.

Tracing the Star's Journey Across Galaxies

To better understand the star's history, the team combined their observations with data from the European Space Agency's Gaia mission. This allowed them to calculate both its distance and its motion through the Milky Way.

By tracing its path backward over billions of years, they determined that the star originated in the Large Magellanic Cloud before eventually being pulled into the Milky Way.

A Rare Chemical Signature and Unexpected Clues

Further analysis revealed another surprising feature. Ji divided the class into groups to study different aspects of the star. Orrantia and Do led the team examining its carbon content, which turned out to be so low that it could not be detected.

"The star has so little carbon that it suggests an early sprinkling of cosmic dust is responsible for making it," said Ji. "This formation pathway has only been seen once before."

A Discovery That Shapes Future Careers

Taking part in such a significant discovery early in their academic careers has influenced the students' future plans. Both Orrantia and Do now intend to pursue graduate studies in astronomy.

"To be able to actually contribute to something like this, it's very exciting," Do said.

"These students have discovered more than just the most pristine star." said Juna Kollmeier, the Director of SDSS-V. "They have discovered their inalienable right to physics. Surveys like SDSS and Gaia make that possible for students of all ages everywhere on Earth and this example shows that there is still plenty of room for discovery."
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific discovery of one of the oldest stars ever found, made by undergraduate students using large-scale astronomical data. This discovery offers meaningful insights into the early universe, involves broad scientific collaboration, and highlights educational impact by engaging students in groundbreaking research.
articles.personality_title: Students discover one of the oldest stars drifting into the Milky Way
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – A group of undergraduate students at the University of Chicago was studying large sets of space data from the Sloan Digital Sky Survey (SDSS), a project that collects information about millions of stars and galaxies to help scientists understand the universe.

**What happened** – During a class project in early 2025, ten students used data from SDSS and traveled to an observatory in Chile to observe stars more closely. They found a star called SDSSJ0715-7334 that is almost entirely made of hydrogen and helium, with very few heavier elements. This star did not form in our Milky Way but in a nearby galaxy called the Large Magellanic Cloud and later moved into our galaxy.

**Impact** – This star is one of the oldest ever found because stars form heavier elements over time, and this one has almost none. It is also the most metal-poor star ever observed, meaning it formed near the dawn of the universe. The discovery helps scientists learn about how stars and galaxies looked billions of years ago. It also shows how students can contribute to real scientific discoveries using big data.

**What's next step** – The students and scientists will continue studying this star and others like it to better understand the early universe. The students involved plan to keep studying astronomy, inspired by their discovery. Big projects like SDSS and the European Space Agency’s Gaia mission will keep providing data for future discoveries.

**One-sentence takeaway** – University students uncovered one of the oldest stars ever seen, revealing new clues about the early universe and showing how big data can lead to exciting discoveries in science.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des étudiants découvrent l’une des plus anciennes étoiles entrant dans la Voie lactée
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Un groupe d’étudiants de premier cycle à l’Université de Chicago étudiait de grandes quantités de données spatiales issues du Sloan Digital Sky Survey (SDSS), un projet qui collecte des informations sur des millions d’étoiles et galaxies pour mieux comprendre l’univers.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Lors d’un projet de classe début 2025, dix étudiants ont utilisé des données du SDSS et se sont rendus dans un observatoire au Chili pour observer des étoiles de plus près. Ils ont trouvé une étoile appelée SDSSJ0715-7334, composée presque uniquement d’hydrogène et d’hélium, avec très peu d’éléments plus lourds. Cette étoile ne s’est pas formée dans notre Voie lactée, mais dans une galaxie voisine, le Grand Nuage de Magellan, puis est entrée dans notre galaxie.

**Impact** – Cette étoile est l’une des plus vieilles jamais trouvées, car les étoiles fabriquent des éléments lourds avec le temps, et celle-ci en contient presque aucun. C’est aussi l’étoile la plus pauvre en métaux jamais observée, ce qui signifie qu’elle s’est formée proche du début de l’univers. Cette découverte aide les scientifiques à mieux comprendre l’apparence des étoiles et galaxies il y a des milliards d’années. Elle montre aussi comment des étudiants peuvent participer à de vraies découvertes scientifiques grâce aux données massives.

**Prochaine étape** – Les étudiants et scientifiques vont continuer à étudier cette étoile et d’autres similaires pour mieux comprendre l’univers primitif. Les étudiants impliqués prévoient de poursuivre leurs études en astronomie, inspirés par cette découverte. Les grands projets comme SDSS et la mission Gaia de l’Agence spatiale européenne continueront à fournir des données pour de futures découvertes.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Des étudiants universitaires ont découvert l’une des plus anciennes étoiles jamais vues, apportant de nouveaux indices sur l’univers primitif et montrant comment les données massives peuvent mener à des découvertes scientifiques passionnantes.
articles.personality_title_es: Estudiantes descubren una de las estrellas más antiguas que entra en la Vía Láctea
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Un grupo de estudiantes universitarios de la Universidad de Chicago estaba estudiando grandes conjuntos de datos del espacio del Sloan Digital Sky Survey (SDSS), un proyecto que recoge información sobre millones de estrellas y galaxias para ayudar a los científicos a entender el universo.

**Qué pasó** – Durante un proyecto de clase a principios de 2025, diez estudiantes usaron datos del SDSS y viajaron a un observatorio en Chile para observar estrellas más de cerca. Encontraron una estrella llamada SDSSJ0715-7334 que está casi completamente hecha de hidrógeno y helio, con muy pocos elementos más pesados. Esta estrella no se formó en nuestra Vía Láctea sino en una galaxia cercana llamada la Gran Nube de Magallanes y después se mudó a nuestra galaxia.

**Impacto** – Esta estrella es una de las más antiguas que se han encontrado porque las estrellas forman elementos más pesados con el tiempo, y esta tiene casi ninguno. También es la estrella con menos metales que se ha observado, lo que significa que se formó cerca del comienzo del universo. El descubrimiento ayuda a los científicos a aprender cómo eran las estrellas y galaxias hace miles de millones de años. También muestra cómo los estudiantes pueden contribuir a descubrimientos científicos reales usando grandes datos.

**Próximo paso** – Los estudiantes y científicos seguirán estudiando esta estrella y otras similares para entender mejor el universo temprano. Los estudiantes involucrados planean continuar estudiando astronomía, inspirados por su descubrimiento. Proyectos grandes como SDSS y la misión Gaia de la Agencia Espacial Europea seguirán proporcionando datos para futuros descubrimientos.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Estudiantes universitarios descubrieron una de las estrellas más antiguas vistas, revelando nuevas pistas sobre el universo temprano y demostrando cómo los grandes datos pueden llevar a emocionantes descubrimientos científicos.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a glowing ancient star composed of soft hydrogen and helium hues, gently drifting from a swirling, distant companion galaxy toward a vast, welcoming Milky Way galaxy rendered with subtle, natural colors, accompanied by an abstract group of student silhouettes observing the star through a telescope under a serene night sky.

articles.title: Meteor impacts may have sparked life on Earth, scientists say
articles.summary: Asteroid impacts may have helped kick-start life on Earth by creating hot, chemical-rich environments ideal for early biology. These impact-generated hydrothermal systems could have lasted thousands of years—long enough for life’s building blocks to form. Scientists now think these environments may have been common on early Earth, making them a strong candidate for where life began. The idea could also guide the search for life on other worlds.
articles.content:
Meteor strikes may have done more than reshape Earth's surface. New research suggests they could have created the hot, chemically rich environments needed for life to begin.

"No one knows, from a scientific perspective, how life could have been formed from an early Earth that had no life," said Shea Cinquemani, who earned her bachelor's degree in marine biology and fisheries management from the Rutgers School of Environmental and Biological Sciences in May 2025. "How does something come from nothing?"

Cinquemani led a scientific review published in the Journal of Marine Science and Engineering that explores where life may have first emerged. The study focuses on hydrothermal vents, where heated, mineral-laden water moves through rock and into surrounding water, creating the right energy and chemistry for complex reactions.

Her analysis highlights hydrothermal systems formed by meteor impacts as an overlooked but potentially important setting for life's beginnings. These environments may have been widespread on early Earth, making them strong candidates for where life first took hold.

From Class Project to Scientific Publication

The study, co-authored with Rutgers oceanographer Richard Lutz, stands out because Cinquemani began the work as an undergraduate assignment that later became a peer-reviewed publication.

"It's amazing," Lutz said. "You often have undergraduates that are part of papers - faculty choose undergraduates all the time to work on papers and projects. But for an undergraduate to be the lead author is a huge deal."

The project began during Cinquemani's senior year in a course called "Hydrothermal Vents," taught by Lutz, a Distinguished Professor in the Department of Marine and Coastal Sciences. Her initial task was to explore whether hydrothermal vents on Mars could have supported life.

"I was like, 'I know nothing about this topic,'" she said. "Thinking about the origins of biology on another planet was like, whoa. Not sure how I'm going to do this." The work pushed her beyond biology into chemistry, physics and geology, she said.

After graduating, she expanded the assignment into a full review comparing deep-sea vents and impact-generated systems. The paper went through an extensive peer-review process.

"I have never seen such a rigorous review process," Lutz said. "There were 15 pages of comments and five different rounds of reviews. She had the patience and perseverance, and the paper turned out magnificently."

Hydrothermal Vents as Cradles of Life

Scientists have long considered deep-sea hydrothermal vents as possible sites where life began. Discovered in the late 1970s, these systems support entire ecosystems in complete darkness.

Instead of relying on sunlight, organisms in these environments use chemical energy from compounds such as hydrogen sulfide. This process, known as chemosynthesis, allows life to thrive without photosynthesis.

Some vents are powered by heat from volcanic activity deep within Earth, while others form through chemical reactions between water and rock that generate heat without magma. In both cases, they create warm, nutrient-rich pockets in the otherwise cold and barren deep ocean.

Impact Craters as Hidden Chemical Factories

Cinquemani's work draws attention to a less-studied type of hydrothermal system formed by meteor impacts.

When a large meteor collides with Earth, it produces intense heat that melts surrounding rock. As the crater cools and fills with water, it can develop into a hot, mineral-rich system similar to deep-sea vents.

"You have a lake surrounding a very, very warm center," Cinquemani said. "And now you get a hydrothermal vent system, just like in the deep sea, but made by the heat from an impact."

To understand how these environments might support life, she examined three well-known impact sites from different periods in Earth's history. The Chicxulub crater beneath Mexico's Yucatán Peninsula formed about 65 million years ago and later hosted a long-lasting hydrothermal system. The Haughton crater in the Canadian Arctic formed around 31 million years ago. Lonar Lake in India, created about 50,000 years ago, still contains water and offers insight into how such systems evolve.

These impact-driven systems can persist for thousands to tens of thousands of years, providing enough time for simple molecules to combine into more complex structures that could eventually lead to life.

A New Perspective on Earth's Early Conditions

Early Earth experienced frequent asteroid impacts, making these environments likely common. While impacts are often associated with destruction, they may also have created conditions suitable for life.

This perspective builds on decades of research into deep-sea vents while expanding the range of possible locations where life could have originated.

Lutz was among the early researchers who explored deep-sea vents when they were first discovered. As a young postdoctoral scientist, he joined pioneering expeditions and descended more than a mile below the ocean surface in the submersible Alvin, where he observed thriving ecosystems in total darkness.

These discoveries helped establish that life can exist without sunlight, reshaping scientific understanding of extreme environments.

"We have talked for many years about the possibility that life may have originated at deep-sea hydrothermal vents," Lutz said.

Cinquemani's research brings together these established ideas with newer evidence suggesting that impact-generated systems may also have played a role and, in some cases, may offer especially favorable conditions for early chemical reactions.

Implications for Life Beyond Earth

The findings may also influence the search for life elsewhere in the solar system. Hydrothermal activity is believed to exist on the ocean floors of icy moons such as Jupiter's Europa and Saturn's Enceladus. Similar systems may have formed in impact craters on early Mars.

If these environments on Earth were capable of supporting the chemistry needed for life, they could guide scientists toward promising places to look beyond our planet.

Driven by Curiosity

For Cinquemani, the research reflects a deeper human drive to understand our origins.

"Humans are insanely curious beings," said Cinquemani, who works as a technician at Rutgers' New Jersey Aquaculture Innovation Center in Cape May, N.J., where she supports aquaculture research while preparing to pursue advanced study in marine science. "We question everything. We may never know exactly how we began, but we can try our best to understand how things might have occurred."
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific advancement in understanding the origins of life on Earth, highlighting the role of meteor impact-generated hydrothermal systems. This discovery has broad implications for biology, geology, and astrobiology, potentially guiding the search for life beyond Earth. It is a well-substantiated, focused study with meaningful impact on scientific knowledge and humanity's understanding of life’s beginnings.
articles.personality_title: Meteor impacts may have created conditions for life on early Earth
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists have long wondered how life began on Earth, especially since early Earth had no life and very different conditions than today. One idea has been that deep-sea hydrothermal vents, which are warm, mineral-rich areas on the ocean floor, could have helped life start.

**What happened** – A new study led by Shea Cinquemani, a recent Rutgers University graduate, suggests that meteor impacts created similar hydrothermal systems by heating rock and water in craters. These systems could have lasted thousands of years, providing the right environment for life’s chemical building blocks to form. The study compared three impact sites, including the famous Chicxulub crater in Mexico, to understand how these systems work.

**Impact** – This research adds a new way to think about where life might have begun on Earth. Instead of just deep-sea vents, meteor impact craters may have been common places for life’s early chemistry. Since early Earth was hit by many asteroids, these impact-generated vents might have been widespread. The study also helps scientists know where to look for signs of life on other planets and moons that have or had similar conditions.

**What's next step** – Researchers will continue studying impact craters and hydrothermal systems on Earth and in space to better understand how life could start. This work may guide future space missions searching for life on moons like Europa or Enceladus, or on Mars, where impact craters exist.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Meteor impacts likely created long-lasting, warm environments on early Earth that helped life’s first ingredients come together, expanding our ideas about where life might begin.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les impacts de météores ont peut-être créé les conditions pour la vie sur la Terre primitive
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques se demandent depuis longtemps comment la vie a commencé sur Terre, surtout que la Terre primitive n’avait pas de vie et des conditions très différentes d’aujourd’hui. Une idée est que les sources hydrothermales profondes, des zones chaudes et riches en minéraux au fond de l’océan, ont pu aider la vie à démarrer.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Une nouvelle étude dirigée par Shea Cinquemani, diplômée récente de l’Université Rutgers, suggère que les impacts de météores ont créé des systèmes hydrothermaux similaires en chauffant la roche et l’eau dans les cratères. Ces systèmes ont pu durer des milliers d’années, offrant l’environnement idéal pour que les premiers composants chimiques de la vie se forment. L’étude a comparé trois sites d’impact, dont le célèbre cratère de Chicxulub au Mexique.

**Impact** – Cette recherche propose une nouvelle façon de penser à l’origine de la vie sur Terre. Au lieu de seulement les sources hydrothermales profondes, les cratères d’impact météoritique pourraient avoir été des lieux fréquents pour la chimie de la vie. Comme la Terre primitive a été frappée par de nombreux astéroïdes, ces systèmes hydrothermaux d’impact auraient pu être très répandus. L’étude aide aussi les scientifiques à savoir où chercher la vie sur d’autres planètes et lunes avec des conditions similaires.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs continueront d’étudier les cratères d’impact et les systèmes hydrothermaux sur Terre et dans l’espace pour mieux comprendre comment la vie pourrait commencer. Ce travail pourrait guider les futures missions spatiales à la recherche de vie sur des lunes comme Europe ou Encelade, ou sur Mars où il existe des cratères d’impact.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Les impacts de météores ont probablement créé des environnements chauds et durables sur la Terre primitive qui ont aidé à rassembler les premiers ingrédients de la vie, élargissant nos idées sur les lieux d’origine de la vie.
articles.personality_title_es: Los impactos de meteoritos podrían haber creado las condiciones para la vida en la Tierra primitiva
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los científicos han preguntado durante mucho tiempo cómo comenzó la vida en la Tierra, especialmente porque la Tierra primitiva no tenía vida y tenía condiciones muy diferentes a las actuales. Una idea es que las fuentes hidrotermales en el fondo del mar, que son áreas cálidas y ricas en minerales, podrían haber ayudado al inicio de la vida.

**Qué pasó** – Un nuevo estudio liderado por Shea Cinquemani, una reciente graduada de la Universidad Rutgers, sugiere que los impactos de meteoritos crearon sistemas hidrotermales similares al calentar la roca y el agua en los cráteres. Estos sistemas pudieron durar miles de años, proporcionando el ambiente adecuado para que se formaran los primeros bloques químicos de la vida. El estudio comparó tres sitios de impacto, incluido el famoso cráter Chicxulub en México.

**Impacto** – Esta investigación ofrece una nueva forma de pensar sobre dónde pudo haber comenzado la vida en la Tierra. En lugar de solo fuentes hidrotermales profundas, los cráteres formados por impactos de meteoritos podrían haber sido lugares comunes para la química de la vida. Como la Tierra primitiva fue golpeada por muchos asteroides, estos sistemas hidrotermales de impacto podrían haber sido muy frecuentes. El estudio también ayuda a los científicos a saber dónde buscar vida en otros planetas y lunas con condiciones similares.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores seguirán estudiando cráteres de impacto y sistemas hidrotermales en la Tierra y en el espacio para entender mejor cómo pudo comenzar la vida. Este trabajo puede guiar futuras misiones espaciales que busquen vida en lunas como Europa o Encélado, o en Marte, donde existen cráteres de impacto.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Los impactos de meteoritos probablemente crearon ambientes cálidos y duraderos en la Tierra primitiva que ayudaron a formar los primeros ingredientes de la vida, ampliando nuestras ideas sobre dónde puede comenzar la vida.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a glowing meteor crater lake at twilight, with steaming mineral-rich hydrothermal vents bubbling gently at its center, surrounded by a serene early Earth landscape under a soft, golden sky, symbolizing the birthplace of life through natural chemical energy and cosmic impact.

articles.title: What Artemis II’s astronauts will look for on the Moon’s far side
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 04 April 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01103-9Mission scientists spoke to Nature about the geological features they’re most excited for the crew to observe.
articles.content:
This Monday, 6 April, if all goes to plan, the astronauts aboard NASA’s Artemis II mission will fly around the far side of the Moon. It will be the first time humans have visited our celestial neighbour since 1972.

Lift off! Artemis II mission sends humans to the Moon — opening a new era of exploration

At mission control at NASA’s Johnson Space Center (JSC) in Houston, all eyes will be on the Moon during the six-hour fly-by, set to begin during the afternoon in Texas. Lunar scientists, in particular, are eager to learn what the astronauts see. The far side of the Moon is markedly different than its near side, which faces Earth; the far side has hardly any of the vast lava flows that mark the near side, but it has a thicker crust and many more impact craters.

On the basis of the Orion spacecraft’s current trajectory, Artemis II scientists in Houston have been updating predictions for what the astronauts will observe during the fly-by (see their latest visualization here). Orion launched into space from Florida on 1 April, and after orbiting Earth to check out the spacecraft’s systems, the astronauts onboard ignited its engines and set course for the Moon the next day.

The Moon and Sun are in a geometry such that only 20% of the lunar far side will be illuminated by sunlight when the astronauts fly by it, according to NASA. That’s a bit of a disappointment because many parts of the lunar far side have never been seen in sunlight by human eyes before, and scientists were hoping for a wider swathe to be visible.

Still, mission scientists are excited for the astronauts to see geological features on the part of the far side that will be illuminated. They gave Nature a sneak peek at these.

Craters galore

Top of many researchers’ lists is the Orientale basin — a 930-kilometre-wide, multi-ringed impact basin in the Moon’s southern hemisphere. It is the largest and the youngest of the many impact craters that formed during a prolonged asteroid blizzard that hit the Moon, known as the Late Heavy Bombardment, beginning around 4 billion years ago. Orientale’s three concentric rings likely formed when a massive asteroid smashed into the Moon, vapourizing material that sloshed outward like a tidal wave and triggered the Moon’s crust to collapse around the point of impact.

Orientale “holds a lot of importance in understanding impact cratering across the Solar System”, says Kelsey Young, Artemis II’s chief lunar scientist. Because it is so large and so detailed, scientists use it as an archetype for understanding how impact basins form on other planets. The basin, which has been imaged previously by robotic probes circling the Moon, lies on the boundary between the lunar near and far sides. So when the Moon wiggles a little bit in its orbit, astronomers on Earth can catch a glimpse of the basin’s edge. But the full glory of Orientale has not really been seen by humans — until, perhaps, now.

Artemis II astronauts Jeremy Hansen (with camera) and Reid Wiseman practice photographing the Moon at Johnson Space Center in Houston, Texas.Credit: NASA/Kelsey Young

“It’s just enormous, super complex, and you could stare at it probably for hours,” says Artemis II astronaut Jeremy Hansen.

Other craters that will be illuminated during the fly-by, and which have not been observed by eye in sunlight before, include the 64-kilometre-wide Ohm crater, which has a central peak poking above lava flows on its floor (and is named after German physicist Georg Ohm, of Ohm’s law fame). The astronauts will also study the 9-kilometre-wide Pierazzo crater, named after Italian-American planetary scientist Elisabetta Pierazzo, who was an expert in impact cratering and died in 2011, at age 47.

The astronauts will look for subtle changes in colour and brightness across the lunar surface, as well as at how the changing sunlight angle affects their views and perceptions of lunar topography. Such dynamic observations can add layers of human perception and insight to the detailed photographs of the lunar surface taken by Moon-orbiting spacecraft, mission scientists say.
articles.reasoning: The article describes the Artemis II mission, the first human lunar fly-by since 1972, which will provide new direct human observations of the Moon's far side. This mission represents a significant milestone in space exploration with broad scientific and public interest, offering meaningful insights into lunar geology and impact cratering that can enhance our understanding of the Solar System.
articles.personality_title: Artemis II astronauts to explore the Moon’s mysterious far side
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – NASA’s Artemis II mission is set to fly humans around the far side of the Moon for the first time since 1972. This side of the Moon is very different from the side facing Earth, with fewer lava plains and many more craters.

**What happened** – On April 6, 2026, the astronauts aboard the Orion spacecraft will fly a six-hour path around the Moon’s far side. The spacecraft launched from Florida on April 1 and reached lunar orbit after checking its systems. Scientists are especially excited about the geological features the crew will see, including the huge Orientale basin, a 930-kilometre-wide crater formed by a massive asteroid impact billions of years ago.

**Impact** – This mission is important because it will provide the first close human views of the Moon’s far side in sunlight. The astronauts will observe craters like Orientale, Ohm, and Pierazzo, which have never been seen by human eyes under sunlight before. Their observations, including changes in color and brightness, will add new insights beyond what robotic probes have captured.

**What's next step** – After the fly-by, scientists will study the astronauts’ photos and reports to better understand the Moon’s history and surface. This data may help improve knowledge about how impact craters form, not only on the Moon but across the Solar System.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Artemis II will give humans their first close look at the Moon’s far side in sunlight, revealing new details about its ancient craters and geology.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les astronautes d’Artemis II exploreront la mystérieuse face cachée de la Lune
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – La mission Artemis II de la NASA doit emmener des humains autour de la face cachée de la Lune pour la première fois depuis 1972. Cette face est très différente de celle visible depuis la Terre, avec moins de plaines de lave et beaucoup plus de cratères.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Le 6 avril 2026, les astronautes à bord du vaisseau Orion effectueront un survol de six heures autour de la face cachée de la Lune. Le vaisseau a décollé de Floride le 1er avril et a atteint l’orbite lunaire après vérification de ses systèmes. Les scientifiques sont surtout impatients de découvrir les formations géologiques, notamment le vaste bassin d’Orientale, un cratère de 930 kilomètres de diamètre formé par l’impact d’un astéroïde il y a des milliards d’années.

**Impact** – Cette mission est importante car elle offrira les premières vues humaines rapprochées de la face cachée de la Lune en pleine lumière. Les astronautes observeront des cratères comme Orientale, Ohm et Pierazzo, jamais vus auparavant sous la lumière du soleil par des humains. Leurs observations, comme les variations de couleur et de luminosité, apporteront de nouvelles informations au-delà de celles des sondes robotiques.

**Prochaine étape** – Après le survol, les scientifiques étudieront les photos et les rapports des astronautes pour mieux comprendre l’histoire et la surface de la Lune. Ces données aideront à améliorer les connaissances sur la formation des cratères d’impact, non seulement sur la Lune mais dans tout le Système solaire.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Artemis II offrira aux humains leur première vue rapprochée de la face cachée de la Lune en lumière du jour, révélant de nouveaux détails sur ses anciens cratères et sa géologie.
articles.personality_title_es: Los astronautas de Artemis II explorarán el misterioso lado oculto de la Luna
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La misión Artemis II de la NASA llevará humanos alrededor del lado oculto de la Luna por primera vez desde 1972. Este lado es muy diferente al que mira hacia la Tierra, con menos llanuras de lava y muchos más cráteres.

**Qué pasó** – El 6 de abril de 2026, los astronautas a bordo de la nave Orion harán un vuelo de seis horas alrededor del lado oculto de la Luna. La nave despegó desde Florida el 1 de abril y llegó a la órbita lunar tras revisar sus sistemas. Los científicos están especialmente emocionados por las formaciones geológicas que verán, como la enorme cuenca Orientale, un cráter de 930 kilómetros formado por el impacto de un asteroide hace miles de millones de años.

**Impacto** – Esta misión es importante porque dará las primeras vistas humanas cercanas del lado oculto de la Luna a la luz del sol. Los astronautas observarán cráteres como Orientale, Ohm y Pierazzo, que nunca antes habían sido vistos por humanos bajo la luz solar. Sus observaciones, incluyendo cambios en color y brillo, aportarán nuevos conocimientos más allá de las imágenes tomadas por sondas robóticas.

**Próximo paso** – Después del vuelo, los científicos estudiarán las fotos e informes de los astronautas para entender mejor la historia y la superficie lunar. Estos datos pueden ayudar a mejorar el conocimiento sobre cómo se forman los cráteres de impacto, no solo en la Luna, sino en todo el Sistema Solar.

**Resumen en una frase** – Artemis II dará a los humanos su primera vista cercana del lado oculto de la Luna a la luz del sol, revelando nuevos detalles sobre sus antiguos cráteres y geología.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a large, multi-ringed lunar impact basin glowing softly under partial sunlight, with delicate shadows highlighting its concentric rings and rugged craters, while a sleek, stylized spacecraft silhouette gracefully arcs around the far side of the Moon against a dark starry background.

articles.title: Dying stars are devouring giant planets, astronomers discover
articles.summary: Dying stars may be wiping out nearby giant planets as they expand into red giants. Astronomers found that these close-in planets become increasingly rare around more evolved stars, suggesting many have already been swallowed. The likely cause is a gravitational tug that drags planets inward until they break apart or fall into the star. It’s a dramatic glimpse into the chaotic final stages of planetary systems.
articles.content:
A new study suggests that aging stars may be wiping out the giant planets that orbit closest to them. The research, led by astronomers at UCL (University College London) and the University of Warwick, provides fresh evidence that these planets can be pulled inward and destroyed as their host stars evolve.

Stars like our Sun eventually run out of hydrogen fuel. When that happens, they begin to cool and expand, entering a phase known as a red giant. Scientists estimate that the Sun will reach this stage in about five billion years.

The new findings, published in the Monthly Notices of the Royal Astronomical Society, are based on observations of nearly half a million stars that have recently entered this "post-main sequence" phase of their life cycle.

Missing Planets Around Red Giants

Researchers identified 130 planets and planet candidates (i.e., that still need to be confirmed) orbiting close to these stars, including 33 that had not been detected before.

However, a clear pattern emerged. Planets in tight orbits were much less common around stars that had expanded enough to become red giants (i.e. that were further on in their post-main sequence evolution). This suggests that many of these close-in planets may have already been destroyed.

Lead author Dr. Edward Bryant (Mullard Space Science Laboratory at UCL and the University of Warwick) explained: "This is strong evidence that as stars evolve off their main sequence they can quickly cause planets to spiral into them and be destroyed. This has been the subject of debate and theory for some time but now we can see the impact of this directly and measure it at the level of a large population of stars.

"We expected to see this effect but we were still surprised by just how efficient these stars seem to be at engulfing their close planets."

The Gravitational Pull That Dooms Planets

The team believes the process is driven by a gravitational tug-of-war between the star and its planet, known as tidal interaction. As a star grows larger, this effect becomes stronger.

Dr. Bryant said: "We think the destruction happens because of the gravitational tug-of-war between the planet and the star, called tidal interaction. As the star evolves and expands, this interaction becomes stronger. Just like the Moon pulls on Earth's oceans to create tides, the planet pulls on the star. These interactions slow the planet down and causing its orbit to shrink, making it spiral inwards until it either breaks apart or falls into the star."

What This Means for Our Solar System

The findings also raise questions about the distant future of our own solar system.

Co-author Dr. Vincent Van Eylen (Mullard Space Science Laboratory at UCL) said: "In a few billion years, our own Sun will enlarge and become a red giant. When this happens, will the solar system planets survive? We are finding that in some cases planets do not.

"Earth is certainly safer than the giant planets in our study, which are much closer to their star. But we only looked at the earliest part of the post-main sequence phase, the first one or two million years of it -- the stars have a lot more evolution to go.

"Unlike the missing giant planets in our study, Earth itself might survive the Sun's red giant phase. But life on Earth probably would not."

How Scientists Found the Planets

To carry out the study, researchers used data from NASA's Transiting Exoplanet Survey Satellite (TESS). They relied on a computer algorithm to detect tiny, repeated dips in starlight that occur when a planet passes in front of its star. The analysis focused on giant planets with short orbital periods (i.e., that took no more than 12 days to orbit their star).

The team started with more than 15,000 possible signals. After applying strict checks to eliminate false positives, they narrowed the list to 130 planets and planet candidates. Among these, 48 were already confirmed, 49 were previously identified as planet candidates (i.e., they still need to be confirmed), and 33 were newly discovered candidates.

Fewer Planets as Stars Evolve

The results show a clear decline in the number of close-in giant planets as stars age. Overall, only 0.28% of the studied stars hosted such planets. Younger post-main sequence stars had a higher rate of 0.35%, similar to stars still on the main sequence. In contrast, more evolved red giants showed a much lower rate of just 0.11%. (For this analysis, the researchers excluded the smallest 12 of the 130 identified planets.)

Using TESS data, scientists can estimate each planet's size (radius). To confirm whether these objects are truly planets rather than planet candidates, astronomers must determine their mass and rule out alternatives such as low-mass stars or brown dwarfs ("failed stars" whose core pressure is not high enough to start nuclear fusion).

This is done by tracking subtle movements in the host star and measuring the gravitational pull exerted by the orbiting object.

Dr. Bryant added: "Once we have these planets' masses, that will help us understand exactly what is causing these planets to spiral in and be destroyed."

The researchers received funding from the UK Science and Technology Facilities Council (STFC).
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific discovery about the fate of giant planets orbiting aging stars, providing new insights into stellar evolution and planetary system dynamics. This has broad implications for understanding the future of our solar system and planetary systems in general, representing a meaningful advancement in space science with substantial educational and curiosity-driven impact.
articles.personality_title: Aging stars swallow nearby giant planets, new study finds
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Stars like our Sun eventually run out of fuel and grow bigger, becoming red giants. Scientists have wondered what happens to the planets close to these aging stars during this dramatic change.

**What happened** – Astronomers from University College London and the University of Warwick studied nearly half a million stars that recently started expanding into red giants. Using data from NASA’s TESS satellite, they found 130 giant planets orbiting close to these stars. But fewer planets were found around the more expanded red giants, showing many close-in planets have already disappeared.

**Impact** – This discovery gives clear proof that as stars grow larger, their gravity pulls nearby planets inward until the planets break apart or fall into the star. It’s the first large-scale evidence of how aging stars can destroy their closest giant planets. This helps us understand the future of planetary systems, including what might happen to our own solar system.

**What’s next step** – Researchers plan to measure the masses of these planets to better understand why and how fast they spiral into their stars. This will improve models of how stars and planets interact as stars age. It might also clarify if smaller planets, like Earth, could survive the Sun’s red giant phase.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Scientists have found strong evidence that aging stars swallow their closest giant planets by pulling them inward as the stars expand.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les étoiles vieillissantes dévorent leurs planètes géantes proches, révèle une nouvelle étude
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les étoiles comme notre Soleil finissent par manquer de carburant et grandissent, devenant des géantes rouges. Les scientifiques se demandent ce qu’il advient des planètes proches de ces étoiles vieillissantes.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des astronomes de l’Université College London et de l’Université de Warwick ont étudié près d’un demi-million d’étoiles qui ont récemment commencé à devenir des géantes rouges. Grâce aux données du satellite TESS de la NASA, ils ont trouvé 130 planètes géantes proches de ces étoiles. Mais moins de planètes ont été détectées autour des géantes rouges plus développées, montrant que beaucoup de planètes proches ont déjà disparu.

**Impact** – Cette découverte prouve clairement que lorsque les étoiles grandissent, leur gravité attire les planètes proches vers elles jusqu’à ce que celles-ci se brisent ou tombent dans l’étoile. C’est la première preuve à grande échelle de la destruction des planètes géantes proches par des étoiles vieillissantes. Cela aide à mieux comprendre le futur des systèmes planétaires, y compris le nôtre.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs prévoient de mesurer la masse de ces planètes pour mieux comprendre pourquoi et à quelle vitesse elles tombent dans leur étoile. Cela améliorera les modèles d’interaction entre étoiles et planètes en vieillissant. Cela pourrait aussi clarifier si des planètes plus petites, comme la Terre, pourraient survivre à la phase de géante rouge du Soleil.

**Phrase clé** – Les scientifiques ont trouvé des preuves fortes que les étoiles vieillissantes avalent leurs planètes géantes proches en les attirant vers elles lorsqu’elles grandissent.
articles.personality_title_es: Estrellas envejecidas devoran planetas gigantes cercanos, revela un nuevo estudio
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Las estrellas como nuestro Sol eventualmente se quedan sin combustible y crecen, convirtiéndose en gigantes rojas. Los científicos se preguntan qué pasa con los planetas cercanos a estas estrellas envejecidas.

**Qué pasó** – Astrónomos de University College London y University of Warwick estudiaron casi medio millón de estrellas que recientemente comenzaron a expandirse en gigantes rojas. Usando datos del satélite TESS de la NASA, encontraron 130 planetas gigantes orbitando cerca de estas estrellas. Pero se encontraron menos planetas alrededor de las gigantes rojas más grandes, mostrando que muchos planetas cercanos ya han desaparecido.

**Impacto** – Este descubrimiento ofrece evidencia clara de que a medida que las estrellas crecen, su gravedad atrae a los planetas cercanos hasta que se rompen o caen dentro de la estrella. Es la primera prueba a gran escala de cómo las estrellas envejecidas pueden destruir sus planetas gigantes más cercanos. Esto ayuda a entender el futuro de los sistemas planetarios, incluido el nuestro.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores planean medir la masa de estos planetas para entender mejor por qué y qué tan rápido caen en sus estrellas. Esto mejorará los modelos de cómo las estrellas y planetas interactúan al envejecer. También podría aclarar si planetas más pequeños, como la Tierra, podrían sobrevivir la fase de gigante roja del Sol.

**Frase clave** – Los científicos han encontrado evidencia fuerte de que las estrellas envejecidas devoran sus planetas gigantes cercanos al atraerlos cuando crecen.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a large glowing red giant star gently expanding and enveloping smaller, glowing orb-like giant planets spiraling inward, surrounded by a soft cosmic backdrop with subtle tidal wave patterns symbolizing gravitational pull, using natural reds, oranges, and deep space blues.
